student_message: It sounds confusing. I don't understand the mirror thing.
final_message:
It is totally okay to feel confused! Math can be really hard sometimes, and I am so proud of you for telling me exactly how you feel. Feeling stuck is just a normal part of learning, and you are doing great! 💛 

Let's forget about mirrors for a minute and think about making a paper butterfly. Imagine you take a piece of paper, fold it right down the middle, and paint one wing on one side. While the paint is still wet, you close the paper along that fold. When you open it back up, the wet paint makes the exact same wing on the other side! Both halves match perfectly. That fold in the middle is called a line of symmetry. Does thinking about a folded painted butterfly make it a little easier to picture? 🦋

orchestrator_intent.goal: Explain reflection symmetry using a simpler, everyday analogy.
orchestrator_intent.reasoning: Riya is confused by the mirror analogy. Dialogue will provide a clearer explanation, while encouragement addresses her lack of confidence.

current_section_title: Reflection Symmetry and Lines of Symmetry
current_section_content: A line of symmetry is a fold line that divides a shape into two halves that are perfect mirror images of each other. Some shapes have a vertical line of symmetry (left-right mirror), some have a horizontal line (top-bottom mirror), and some have both. You can use this idea to cut out shapes by drawing only half or one-quarter of the shape on folded paper, then cutting and unfolding. Letters like A have one vertical line of symmetry, while letters like H have both vertical and horizontal lines of symmetry.
